# Adapting DANCE from EEG to ECG

## Executive summary

DANCE is not fundamentally an EEG-only *architecture*. It is an event-set detector that takes a multichannel time series, compresses it with a convolutional encoder plus Perceiver bottleneck, and decodes a sparse set of time-localised events with a DETR-style query decoder. The parts that are genuinely EEG-specific sit lower in the stack: the data layer in neuralset, the use of electrode positions through ChannelMerger, the label schemas chosen in the paper, and the evaluation metrics used for EEG tasks. In blunt terms, the model is portable, but the data plumbing is not. [\[1\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

The cleanest ECG migration path is **not** to rewrite the whole repository around ECG from day one. The pragmatic path is to make ECG datasets emit the **same batch contract** that DANCE already expects, initially even keeping the legacy key names if necessary, then disabling ChannelMerger for ECG unless you explicitly build an ECG lead-geometry substitute. That gets you to a working baseline fastest and avoids breaking the matcher, losses, metrics plumbing, and trainer before you have any ECG results at all. [\[2\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

For ECG, DANCE fits some problem formulations much better than others. It is a strong fit for **rhythm episode detection** and **wave delineation** with explicit temporal annotations. It is an acceptable fit for **beat-event detection/classification** if you convert point annotations into short spans or replace IoU-based scoring with tolerance-based matching. It is a weak fit for pure **record-level diagnosis** datasets such as PTB-XL or MIMIC-IV-ECG unless you deliberately reformulate the task into one or a few whole-window events. [\[3\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/metrics.py)

One hard architectural constraint from the paper matters a lot for ECG: the Perceiver always outputs **256 latent tokens** per window. The paper’s own heuristic shows that event localisation breaks down when the shortest event gets too few tokens, with Broderick’s speech dataset at about **3.4 tokens per event** representing the lower validated end of the range. That means ECG window sizes must be chosen from the **shortest event you care about**, not from convenience. For a 100 ms QRS, 8 s windows give about 3.2 tokens per QRS, which is borderline; 3 to 6 s windows are safer for delineation, while much longer windows are only appropriate for long rhythm episodes such as AF or VT. This is an inference from the paper’s token-budget rule and the repo’s fixed 256-latent encoder. [\[4\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1)

This report uses the following assumptions. They are design assumptions, not claims about the paper. First, the target ECG taxonomy is open-ended, so I analyse three sensible variants: **wave delineation**, **beat-level events**, and **rhythm episodes**. Second, the goal is to keep DANCE’s **end-to-end event detection** framing rather than collapsing ECG into tabular HRV features. Third, I prioritise the most production-useful migration path over the prettiest refactor.

## What the paper and repository actually do

The paper formulates the task as **direct event detection from raw, unaligned windows**. Each event is represented as a tuple of normalised start, end, and class, and the model predicts a *set* of such events in one forward pass rather than assigning a single label to the whole window or producing only dense per-sample labels. Across the paper, DANCE is trained separately on **10 heterogeneous EEG datasets** spanning motor imagery, P300, speech/listening, seizure, artefacts, and typing, representing six broader modality/task families. [\[5\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1)

Architecturally, DANCE combines a convolutional frontend, a Perceiver bottleneck, and a DETR-style decoder. In the public code, the default encoder is a SimpleConv stack with hidden size **512**, depth **10**, kernel size **9**, dilation growth **2.5**, initial linear projection **256**, and an optional ChannelMerger that projects channels to **270 virtual channels** using a **2048-dimensional Fourier embedding** with dropout **0.2**. The encoder output dimension is **128**. The Perceiver bottleneck uses **256 latents**, depth **6**, **2** cross-attention heads, **2** latent-attention heads, and head dimensions **64**. The decoder is a TransformerEncoder-based DETR-style decoder with hidden dimension **256**, default depth **4**, **4** heads, dropout **0.1**, and dataset-specific numbers of learnable event queries. It predicts class logits and four scalar interval heads: start, end, centre, and duration. [\[6\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/models/encoder.py)

The loss stack is hybrid. First, a **Hungarian matcher** performs one-to-one query-target matching using a cost that combines class probability and interval overlap. Then DetrLoss applies cross-entropy to matched and unmatched queries plus an interval IoU loss on matched pairs. In parallel, DANCE adds a **dense per-timestep cross-entropy head** and a **consistency KL term** that forces agreement between the dense head and a dense probability map rendered back from the sparse DETR predictions. In the public implementation, the weights are weight\_class=1.0, weight\_iou=5.0, weight\_dense=1.0, and weight\_consistency=0.5. [\[7\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/dance.py)

The training pipeline in the released configs is straightforward and surprisingly conservative. The optimiser is **AdamW** with learning rate **5e-5** and weight decay **1e-3**. The scheduler is **OneCycleLR** with max\_lr=5e-5 and a warm-up phase over the first **10%** of training. The default trainer runs for **100 epochs**, monitors val\_f1\_event, and uses patience **20**; TUSZ is the exception, using **25 epochs**, effectively no early stopping, and monitoring val\_f1\_sample. Subject-grouped **5-fold CV** is used in the generic splitter, while TUSZ uses the official fixed train/dev/eval split. The paper also reports that all models were trained on a single **NVIDIA V100 GPU** except seizure detection, which used **8 GPUs** with distributed data parallelism. [\[8\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/defaults.yaml)

Signal preprocessing in the public configs is more important than it looks. The default EegExtractor runs at **128 Hz**, applies a **0.1 to 100 Hz** filter, uses a **RobustScaler**, and clamps amplitudes at **16**. Event supervision is built by EventEncoder, which can encode dense class maps or sparse event starts, ends, and classes. The feature frequencies vary by dataset and correspond to the dense/token supervision rate, not the raw sensor extraction rate. For the Temple datasets, the code explicitly cleans channel-specific annotations: TUSZ seizures are deduplicated across channels, generic background and generic seizure rows are removed, known-bad timelines are dropped, and near-duplicate events are removed before training. The paper also states that TUAR and TUSZ annotations are projected from channel-specific annotations onto a single global timeline because DANCE targets global event detection. [\[9\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/defaults.yaml)

I did **not** find any explicit data augmentation policy in the released dance configs. The default configs and per-dataset YAMLs expose preprocessing, splitting, optimiser, losses, and metrics, but no augmentation section is configured in the public release. That matters for ECG because you should assume you will need to design this part yourself rather than inheriting a proven augmentation recipe from the EEG codebase. [\[10\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/defaults.yaml)

Evaluation is done with two complementary F1 scores. F1-sample is a **per-timestep multilabel macro F1** computed from dense masks derived from events. F1-event is a **class-aware event-span F1** at **IoU \> 0.5**, with predictions ranked greedily by confidence, matched one time each to targets, and scored as true positives only if class and overlap both agree. The released README reports reference results for the three public examples: BI2014a P300 F1-sample 0.651 ± 0.005, F1-event 0.525 ± 0.003; BNCI2014\_001 motor imagery 0.551 ± 0.019, 0.462 ± 0.021; and TUSZ seizure 0.255 ± 0.010, 0.248 ± 0.044. [\[11\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

The dependency stack is fairly lean: Python **\>=3.12**, neuralset\>=0.2.1, neuraltrain\>=0.2.2, neuralfetch\>=0.2.1, lightning\>=2.0.8, x\_transformers==1.44.4, perceiver\_pytorch, torchmetrics\>=1.1.2, pydantic\>=2.5.0, submitit, exca\>=0.5.20, scikit-learn\>=1.0, pandas, and numpy, with optional wandb\>=0.15.11. There is one production-grade caveat here: matcher.py imports scipy.optimize.linear\_sum\_assignment, but scipy is not declared explicitly in pyproject.toml. In practice you may get it transitively, but that is sloppy and should be fixed before any serious ECG fork. [\[12\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/pyproject.toml)

## Original EEG datasets used by the authors

The released repository only exposes **three runnable example dataset configs**: BI2014a, BNCI2014\_001, and TUSZ. The paper, however, evaluates on **ten** datasets. That means a fully precise replication inventory cannot be reconstructed from the public repo alone; some dataset-level details are explicit in the paper, some in public dataset pages, and some remain unresolved from the sources I could verify. I am calling those gaps out directly instead of pretending the evidence is complete. [\[13\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

### High-confidence inventory

| Dataset | DANCE task and labels/events | Official source | EEG only or multimodal | Raw signals available | Event annotations available | Sample rate, channels, format | Access and licence status | Preprocessing / handling used by DANCE |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **BNCI2014\_001** | Motor imagery. DANCE labels: left\_hand, right\_hand, feet, tongue. Paper shorthand: BNCI2014. Window **8 s**, **5** queries. [\[14\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/tangermann2012.yaml) | MOABB dataset page and BCI Competition IV dataset 2a page. [\[15\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BNCI2014_001.html) | **Multimodal/auxiliary**: EEG plus **3 EOG** channels in the original competition release. [\[16\]](https://www.bbci.de/competition/iv/) | Yes, public competition data via MOABB/BCI Competition package. [\[15\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BNCI2014_001.html) | Yes, cue-based trial labels are provided. [\[15\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BNCI2014_001.html) | **250 Hz**, **22 EEG \+ 3 EOG**. The retrieved source does not explicitly expose the exact on-disk file format. [\[16\]](https://www.bbci.de/competition/iv/) | Public competition dataset. The retrieved page does **not** state a licence explicitly. [\[16\]](https://www.bbci.de/competition/iv/) | DANCE config: feature frequency **32 Hz**, window **8 s**, **5** queries. Global defaults imply sensor extraction at **128 Hz**, filter **0.1–100 Hz**, RobustScaler, clamp **16**, subject-grouped k-fold split. [\[17\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/tangermann2012.yaml) |
| **BI2014b** | P300 / ERP. Labels: Target, NonTarget. Window **8 s**, **150** queries. [\[18\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | MOABB BI2014b page. [\[19\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | EEG only. [\[19\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | Yes. [\[19\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | Yes, target and non-target event tags are present. [\[19\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | **512 Hz**, **32 wet EEG channels**, **1 s** trials. The MOABB page exposes the experimental metadata but not the exact packaged file format in the retrieved lines. [\[19\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | **CC BY 4.0** according to MOABB. [\[19\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | DANCE paper window **8 s** and **150** queries. If using repo defaults, raw extraction is still normalised to **128 Hz** with the shared EEG preprocessing stack unless overridden elsewhere. [\[20\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) |
| **TUSZ** | Seizure detection/classification. Paper labels: CT, Focal, Generalized, Absence. Repo mapping collapses raw TUH codes fnsz/cpsz/spsz/tnsz \-\> 1, gnsz/mysz \-\> 2, absz \-\> 3, tcsz \-\> 4. Window **256 s**, **10** queries. [\[21\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/shah2018.yaml) | TUH EEG landing and download pages. [\[22\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/tuh_eeg/) | EEG only clinical corpus. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | Yes. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | Yes, manual seizure annotations with **start, stop, channel, seizure type**. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | The current TUH page does not expose one fixed sample rate or channel count because TUSZ is a clinical subset of TUEG. DANCE therefore standardises extraction through its own EEG extractor. [\[24\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | Registration-controlled TUH access via SSH keys and rsync. The retrieved source does not expose the legal text as a standard open licence. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | DANCE uses the official fixed split, **50%** training overlap, seizure-weighted sampling, and the SeizurePreprocessor that deduplicates channel-specific labels, removes bckg and generic seiz, drops bad timelines, and removes near-duplicate overlaps. Sparse labels are encoded at **1 Hz**. [\[25\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/shah2018.yaml) |
| **TUAR** | Artefact detection. DANCE labels: Chew, Elpp, Eye Mov, Musc, Shiver. Window **16 s**, **25** queries. [\[26\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | TUH download page. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | EEG only clinical corpus. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | Yes. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | Yes, channel-specific artefact annotations. [\[27\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | The retrieved source does not expose a single fixed sample rate or fixed channel count. TUAR is a TUEG subset with labels for five artefact categories. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | Registration-controlled TUH access. Explicit licence text was not present in the retrieved lines. [\[23\]](https://isip.piconepress.com/projects/nedc/html/tuh_eeg/) | The paper states channel-specific artefacts are projected to a single global timeline. Shared EEG preprocessing defaults still apply unless overridden elsewhere. [\[28\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) |
| **Broderick et al. dataset** | Speech / listening. DANCE labels it as **“Phonemes (Voice Detection)”**. Window **8 s**, **150** queries. [\[29\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Dryad record for “Electrophysiological correlates of semantic dissimilarity reflect the comprehension of natural, narrative speech”. [\[30\]](https://datadryad.org/dataset/doi%3A10.5061/dryad.070jc) | **Multimodal**: EEG plus speech stimulus packages and derived event information. [\[30\]](https://datadryad.org/dataset/doi%3A10.5061/dryad.070jc) | Yes, downloadable packages on Dryad totalling **14.84 GB**. [\[30\]](https://datadryad.org/dataset/doi%3A10.5061/dryad.070jc) | Yes, DANCE clearly uses time-locked speech-derived events, but the exact phoneme-alignment schema is not spelled out on the retrieved Dryad lines. [\[31\]](https://datadryad.org/dataset/doi%3A10.5061/dryad.070jc) | Exact sample rate, channel count, and on-disk format were **not exposed** in the retrieved source. [\[30\]](https://datadryad.org/dataset/doi%3A10.5061/dryad.070jc) | Open Dryad download; I did not retrieve a dataset-specific licence line and therefore will not invent one. [\[30\]](https://datadryad.org/dataset/doi%3A10.5061/dryad.070jc) | DANCE window **8 s**, **150** queries. The paper’s token-budget table reports average event duration about **0.08 s** and about **75.3 events per window**, making this one of the shortest-event regimes used in the benchmark. [\[32\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) |
| **SparrKULee** | Speech / listening. DANCE labels it as **“Words (Voice Detection)”**. Window **8 s**, **50** queries. [\[29\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | KU Leuven research data repository record and the dataset citation in the paper. [\[33\]](https://rdr.kuleuven.be/dataset.xhtml%3Bjsessionid%3Dc178b8fbd7ad3049a8b2b6c4d863?fileAccess=Public&fileSortField=name&fileSortOrder=desc&fileTag=%22Data%22&folderPresort=true&persistentId=doi%3A10.48804%2FK3VSND&q=&tagPresort=false&version=) | **Multimodal**: EEG plus speech stimulus material. [\[34\]](https://rdr.kuleuven.be/dataset.xhtml%3Bjsessionid%3Dc178b8fbd7ad3049a8b2b6c4d863?fileAccess=Public&fileSortField=name&fileSortOrder=desc&fileTag=%22Data%22&folderPresort=true&persistentId=doi%3A10.48804%2FK3VSND&q=&tagPresort=false&version=) | At least official EEG data are available; the retrieved snippet refers specifically to the **officially provided preprocessed data downsampled to 64 Hz**. Whether raw unprocessed EEG is also public was not explicit in the retrieved lines. [\[35\]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/382615162_SparrKULee_A_Speech-Evoked_Auditory_Response_Repository_from_KU_Leuven_Containing_the_EEG_of_85_Participants) | Yes, DANCE uses word/voice-detection events, so time-aligned event annotations exist in some form. [\[36\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Retrieved metadata show **64-channel EEG**, **85 subjects**, and **90–150 minutes** of natural speech per participant; the snippet I retrieved reports use of **64 Hz preprocessed data** in downstream evaluation. [\[35\]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/382615162_SparrKULee_A_Speech-Evoked_Auditory_Response_Repository_from_KU_Leuven_Containing_the_EEG_of_85_Participants) | Official repository exists; specific licence text was not exposed in the retrieved snippets. [\[34\]](https://rdr.kuleuven.be/dataset.xhtml%3Bjsessionid%3Dc178b8fbd7ad3049a8b2b6c4d863?fileAccess=Public&fileSortField=name&fileSortOrder=desc&fileTag=%22Data%22&folderPresort=true&persistentId=doi%3A10.48804%2FK3VSND&q=&tagPresort=false&version=) | DANCE window **8 s**, **50** queries. The paper reports average event duration about **0.30 s** and **19.5 events per window**. [\[32\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) |

### Datasets present in the paper but not fully reconstructable from the retrieved public sources

| Dataset | What is explicit in the paper/repo | What remains unresolved from the retrieved sources |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **BI2013** | P300 task; labels Target vs Non Target; window **32 s**; **150** queries. [\[29\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Exact official download page, licence, sample rate, channel count, and file format were not recovered here. |
| **BI2014a** | P300 task; labels Target vs Non Target; window **32 s**; **150** queries. The repo tutorial config comment says “visual P300, **64 subjects**”. [\[37\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/korczowski2014a.yaml) | The official Zenodo page linked from the repo rate-limited during retrieval, so I could not verify sample rate, channel count, or explicit licence from primary lines. \[38\] |
| **BNCI2015** | Motor imagery task; paper labels R. Hand, Feet; window **32 s**; **40** queries. [\[29\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Exact public identifier, channel/spec metadata, and licence were not explicit in the retrieved public repo sources. |
| **Pinet** | Typing task; paper labels Key Press; window **8 s**; **100** queries. [\[39\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Exact official dataset page, sample rate, channel count, public access status, and event annotation format were not recovered from the retrieved sources. |

The most important take-away from the inventory is not just “these are ten EEG datasets”. It is that the benchmark mixes **very different temporal regimes**: speech and typing with dense short events, P300 with many one-second stimulus events, motor imagery with relatively long sparse intervals, and clinical artefacts/seizures with long overlapping events. That diversity is exactly why ECG migration must start from a **clear ECG event taxonomy** rather than with a blind “replace EEG with ECG” rename. [\[40\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1)

## How EEG events map to ECG events

The common EEG event families used in DANCE are not equally transferable to ECG. Some map well. Some do not. If you ignore this and try to preserve the EEG task mix, you will build a nonsense benchmark.

| EEG event family used in DANCE | Example datasets in paper | ECG analogue | Compatibility judgement |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Clinical episode events** | TUSZ seizures; TUAR artefacts. [\[41\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Rhythm episodes such as AF, AFL, VT, SVT, bradycardia, pauses; waveform/noise artefacts such as motion, lead-off, baseline wander, EMG, powerline. | **Strong match.** This is the cleanest ECG translation. |
| **Short sensory/evoked events** | P300 Target vs NonTarget in BI2013/BI2014a/BI2014b. [\[42\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | There is no true intrinsic cardiac equivalent. A practical substitute would be normal vs abnormal beats or paced vs intrinsic beats, but that changes the semantics completely. | **Weak semantic match, workable engineering match.** |
| **Long endogenous state intervals** | Motor imagery classes in BNCI2014/BNCI2015. [\[14\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/tangermann2012.yaml) | Sustained rhythm states such as AF burden intervals, sleep-stage-related rhythm changes, exercise-test phases. | **Moderate engineering match, poor physiological equivalence.** |
| **Dense linguistic or behaviour-linked events** | Broderick phoneme/voice events, SparrKULee word/voice events, Pinet key presses. [\[43\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Beat-level events such as R-peaks, beat classes, or wave boundaries. | **Good temporal-structure match, poor semantic match.** Useful if your goal is testing whether DANCE can handle many short sparse events, not if you want ECG tasks analogous to speech or typing. |

The right ECG taxonomies are therefore these three, and you should choose **one** for the first migration rather than mixing all three.

First, **wave delineation**: P-onset/P-peak/P-offset, QRS-onset/R-peak/QRS-offset, T-onset/T-peak/T-offset. This is the closest ECG equivalent to DANCE’s short, numerous, highly localised event regime. LUDB is the best starting point here. [\[44\]](https://physionet.org/content/ludb/)

Second, **beat-event detection/classification**: normal beats, PACs, PVCs, paced beats, fusion beats, and perhaps ventricular escape beats depending on the dataset. This is close to the typing and short speech-event regimes in terms of event density, but the current IoU-based event metric becomes awkward if your source annotations are point-like rather than interval-like. [\[45\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/metrics.py)

Third, **rhythm episode detection**: AF, AFL, VT, bigeminy/trigeminy segments, pauses, bradycardia/tachycardia intervals. This is the most natural ECG analogue to TUSZ seizures and TUAR artefacts and is the place where DANCE’s original event-span design carries over most cleanly. CPSC2021 and large continuous datasets such as Icentia11k are strong candidates here. [\[46\]](https://physionet.org/content/cpsc2021/1.0.0/)

The incompatibilities matter. There is **no serious ECG equivalent** to “left-hand imagery vs tongue imagery”, and there is **no intrinsic cardiac equivalent** to “target vs non-target visual oddball stimuli”. If your goal is rigorous ECG modelling rather than cute cross-modal symmetry, do not waste time trying to preserve those semantics. Replace them with ECG-native tasks. That is the right scientific move.

## Step-by-step code adaptation plan

The repo is built so that the model core expects a batch dictionary with a raw multichannel signal tensor, optional channel positions, and sparse event targets. In the public Dance module that contract is {"eeg": (B, C, T), "channel\_positions": (B, C, 2), "start": ..., "end": ..., "class": ...}, with background class 0 and optional dense targets. The trainer stack renames event features into the exact target names the matcher and losses expect. [\[2\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

### What to change in the codebase

| Module | What it does now | What to change for ECG |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| dance/dance.py | Expects batch key "eeg" and optional "channel\_positions", then runs encoder, decoder, dense head, and losses. [\[47\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/dance.py) | For the first pass, **do not rename anything**. Feed ECG under the existing "eeg" key to avoid unnecessary breakage. Later, if you care about semantics, add a generic "signal" alias. |
| dance/models/encoder.py | Uses ChannelMerger by default, which was designed for spatially arranged neuro channels and projects to 270 virtual channels. [\[48\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/models/encoder.py) | Start with use\_channel\_merger=False, especially for 1-lead and 2-lead ECG. For 12-lead ECG, only re-enable a merger if you create an ECG-specific lead geometry or learned lead embedding scheme. |
| dance/training/data.py | Builds dataloaders from a neuralset.events.Study, adds ChannelPositions, constructs segments, and optionally uses seizure-weighted sampling. [\[49\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/training/data.py) | Replace the study and signal extractor layer with ECG-native studies. Keep the same segment-and-collate logic. Add an ECG-specific weighted sampler if rhythm episodes are rare. |
| dance/training/extractors.py | EventEncoder supports dense\_class, event\_start, event\_end, and event\_class, with label fields hard-coded for Stimulus, Artifact, and Seizure. [\[50\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/training/extractors.py) | Extend \_LABEL\_FIELD and the event classes so it can encode cardiac event types such as Beat, Rhythm, or Wave. This is a real blocker, not a cosmetic change. |
| dance/training/transforms.py | Contains SeizurePreprocessor for channel-specific TUSZ cleaning. [\[51\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/training/transforms.py) | Add EcgPreprocessor to merge duplicate multi-lead annotations, convert point annotations to spans if needed, merge contiguous rhythm episodes, and map vendor-specific labels into your chosen schema. |
| dance/metrics.py and dance/utils.py | Event F1 is IoU-based; dense/sample F1 is derived from masks rendered from spans. Prediction post-processing uses softmax, argmax, and drops background class 0. [\[52\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/metrics.py) | Keep these only for interval-style ECG tasks. For beat detection, add tolerance-based matching such as ±30 ms or ±50 ms centre tolerance and onset/offset MAE. |
| dance/configs/defaults.yaml and dataset YAMLs | Shared EEG preprocessing and dataset-specific event mappings. [\[10\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/defaults.yaml) | Create ECG dataset YAMLs with ECG extractor name, ECG frequencies, ECG label mappings, shorter windows for beat/wave tasks, and task-appropriate n\_queries. |
| pyproject.toml | Declares core dependencies but omits an explicit SciPy dependency although matcher.py imports SciPy. [\[12\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/pyproject.toml) | Add scipy explicitly. If you are doing production training, leaving this implicit is asking for breakage. |

### Data format conversion

Your ECG dataset wrapper should emit this conceptual contract:

| Key | Shape | Meaning |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| eeg | (B, C, T) | ECG signal tensor. Keep the legacy key initially. |
| channel\_positions | (B, C, 2\) or omitted | Optional. Use only if you intentionally design ECG lead geometry. |
| start, end | (B, max\_events) | Normalised event boundaries in \[0, 1\]. |
| class | (B, max\_events) | Integer event class IDs, with 0 as background/padding. |
| dense | (B, 256\) or (B, 1, 256\) | Optional dense token targets if you choose to precompute them. |

This is important: if your ECG source gives **point events** such as R-peaks, DANCE’s current objective is not mathematically well-matched because IoU on zero-width intervals is degenerate. You have three realistic options. One, convert points into small intervals centred on the point. Two, swap the localisation loss from IoU to centre-distance or tolerance-aware loss. Three, keep IoU only for rhythm-episode or wave-boundary tasks where intervals are inherent. Option one is the quickest, option three is the cleanest scientifically. [\[53\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/losses.py)

### Sampling rate and windowing policy

Do not blindly keep the EEG defaults. The repo’s shared EEG extractor runs at **128 Hz**, which is acceptable for coarse EEG events but can be too low for ECG morphology depending on the task. ECG migration should use **task-driven resampling**:

* For **wave delineation**, use **250 to 500 Hz** and short windows such as **2 to 6 s**.

* For **beat-event detection**, use **250 to 360 Hz** and windows around **3 to 8 s**.

* For **rhythm episodes**, you can go lower in effective supervision frequency and longer in window size, such as **30 to 120 s**, but only if the shortest event still gets enough latent tokens.

This recommendation is an engineering inference from the paper’s token-budget logic and the fixed 256-latent bottleneck, not an explicit instruction in the paper. [\[4\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1)

### Channel handling

ECG channel handling is one of the easiest places to overcomplicate the port.

For **single-lead** or **two-lead** ECG, set use\_channel\_merger=False. The default ChannelMerger was built for spatially arranged EEG electrodes and projecting a 1- or 2-lead ECG into 270 virtual channels is exactly the kind of fashionable nonsense that wastes compute without adding signal. [\[54\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

For **12-lead** ECG, you have two sensible options. The conservative option is still to disable the merger and let the conv stack learn from the raw lead channels directly. The more ambitious option is to replace EEG scalp coordinates with a simple lead embedding or pseudo-geometry over the standard 12-lead system. That can be useful, but it is a phase-two enhancement, not a prerequisite for a valid ECG baseline.

### Augmentation policy for ECG

Because the public DANCE configs do not expose an augmentation recipe, you need to define one yourself. The right augmentations for ECG are not the same as for EEG.

Recommended ECG augmentations for a first serious baseline:

* low-amplitude Gaussian noise

* baseline wander injection

* powerline interference at local mains frequency

* gain scaling

* short time masking

* random lead dropout for 12-lead ECG

* motion/electrode artefact injection if the task includes real-world ambulatory noise

Augmentations to avoid or use very cautiously:

* aggressive time warping that distorts interval physiology

* arbitrary frequency shifts that alter heart-rate semantics

* channel shuffling, which destroys lead identity

* scalp-style spatial dropout logic imported from EEG without adaptation

The paper gives you no public evidence that its EEG performance depended on augmentation, so do not assume you can copy-paste a hidden recipe from the release. [\[10\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/defaults.yaml)

### Loss and label adjustments

For **wave delineation** and **rhythm episodes**, the existing hybrid loss stack is still defensible: Hungarian matching plus class loss plus interval IoU plus dense consistency. For **beat detection**, you should strongly consider either:

* converting beats to short spans and keeping the current loss stack for speed of implementation, or

* replacing IoU with a point-tolerance or centre-distance term for correctness.

For imbalanced ECG tasks, especially AF or rare ectopy, add either **class weights** to cross-entropy or upgrade to a **focal-style class loss**. The public code uses standard cross-entropy everywhere, which is fine for a benchmark but not sacred. [\[55\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/losses.py)

### Metric changes

The original metrics are not terrible, but they are not enough for ECG.

Use these instead:

* **Wave delineation**: onset and offset MAE, tolerance-based sensitivity and PPV, plus event F1.

* **Beat detection/classification**: tolerance-based beat F1, sensitivity, PPV, confusion matrix by beat type.

* **Rhythm episode detection**: episode-level F1/IoU, onset delay, offset delay, and burden error over the whole recording.

* Keep **sample-based dense F1** only when you genuinely have meaningful dense class targets.

If you keep only the original F1-event @ IoU\>0.5 for R-peaks, you are grading the wrong skill. [\[52\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/metrics.py)

### A practical migration timeline

| ![Rendered Mermaid diagram 1][image1] |
| :---: |

### Minimal reproducible example outline

The fastest proof-of-concept is to keep the internal key names stable and adapt only the dataloader and config layer first.

from \_\_future\_\_ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass  
from typing import Final

import numpy as np  
import torch  
from torch import Tensor  
from torch.utils.data import Dataset

@dataclass(frozen=True)  
class EcgEvent:  
    start\_s: float  
    end\_s: float  
    class\_id: int

Batch \= dict\[str, Tensor\]

class EcgWindowDataset(Dataset\[Batch\]):  
    """Minimal ECG-to-DANCE adapter.

    Notes  
    \-----  
    Keep the legacy key names (\`eeg\`, \`start\`, \`end\`, \`class\`) at first.  
    That lets the public DANCE module run without invasive refactoring.  
    """

    def \_\_init\_\_(  
        self,  
        windows: list\[np.ndarray\],  
        events: list\[list\[EcgEvent\]\],  
        \*,  
        duration\_s: float,  
        max\_events: int,  
    ) \-\> None:  
        self.windows: list\[np.ndarray\] \= windows  \# each: (C, T), float32  
        self.events: list\[list\[EcgEvent\]\] \= events  
        self.duration\_s: float \= duration\_s  
        self.max\_events: int \= max\_events

    def \_\_len\_\_(self) \-\> int:  
        return len(self.windows)

    def \_\_getitem\_\_(self, index: int) \-\> Batch:  
        signal\_np: np.ndarray \= self.windows\[index\].astype(np.float32, copy=False)  
        evs: list\[EcgEvent\] \= self.events\[index\]\[: self.max\_events\]

        start: np.ndarray \= np.zeros((self.max\_events,), dtype=np.float32)  
        end: np.ndarray \= np.zeros((self.max\_events,), dtype=np.float32)  
        cls: np.ndarray \= np.zeros((self.max\_events,), dtype=np.int64)

        for i, ev in enumerate(evs):  
            start\[i\] \= np.clip(ev.start\_s / self.duration\_s, 0.0, 1.0)  
            end\[i\] \= np.clip(ev.end\_s / self.duration\_s, 0.0, 1.0)  
            cls\[i\] \= ev.class\_id

        return {  
            "eeg": torch.from\_numpy(signal\_np),   \# legacy name on purpose  
            "start": torch.from\_numpy(start),  
            "end": torch.from\_numpy(end),  
            "class": torch.from\_numpy(cls),  
        }

A first-pass model instantiation should look like this:

from \_\_future\_\_ import annotations

import torch  
from dance import Dance

model: Dance \= Dance(  
    n\_channels=12,          \# or 1 / 2 for ambulatory ECG  
    n\_classes=9,            \# background \+ ECG event classes  
    n\_queries=128,          \# tune to worst-case events per window  
    duration=4.0,           \# example for short-wave delineation  
    use\_channel\_merger=False,  
)

optimizer \= torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=5e-5, weight\_decay=1e-3)

If you integrate ECG into the CLI/config pipeline, the working command shape should remain:

dance run ludb\_ecg \--local \--folder runs/ludb\_ecg

and the critical dataset YAML fields are the study name, raw extractor, window duration, feature\_start, feature\_end, feature\_class, dense\_target, and decoder\_config.n\_queries. That mirrors the repo’s existing dataset integration path. [\[56\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

## ECG datasets suitable as replacements

### Shortlist with suitability ratings

| ECG dataset | Best DANCE use-case | What the retrieved source confirms | Access and licence status | Suitability |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **LUDB** | Wave delineation | PhysioNet states LUDB contains **marked boundaries and peaks of P and T waves and QRS complexes**, with **200** **10-second** **12-lead** ECG records. [\[44\]](https://physionet.org/content/ludb/) | Open PhysioNet project page. I did not retrieve the explicit licence line, so verify it on the current project page before redistribution. [\[44\]](https://physionet.org/content/ludb/) | **Very high** for first ECG port. Best event-annotation fit. |
| **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** | Beat-event detection/classification | PhysioNet confirms **48 half-hour excerpts** of **two-channel** ambulatory ECG from **47 subjects**. [\[57\]](https://physionet.org/content/mitdb/1.0.0/) | Open PhysioNet project page. Explicit licence line not retrieved here. [\[57\]](https://physionet.org/content/mitdb/1.0.0/) | **High** for classic continuous beat tasks, but small by modern deep-learning standards. |
| **CPSC2021** | AF episode detection | PhysioNet states the task is **searching AF episodes in dynamic ECG records** and that the database was built for **PAF event detection**. [\[58\]](https://physionet.org/content/cpsc2021/1.0.0/) | Open challenge dataset on PhysioNet and Zenodo mirror. Explicit licence line not retrieved here. [\[58\]](https://physionet.org/content/cpsc2021/1.0.0/) | **Very high** if your target is span detection for long rhythm episodes. |
| **Icentia11k** | Large-scale beat and rhythm event learning | PhysioNet states it contains **11 thousand patients**, **continuous raw single-lead ECG**, up to **2 weeks**, sampled at **250 Hz**, with **2 billion labelled beats** and rhythm labels including **AF** and **atrial flutter**. [\[59\]](https://physionet.org/content/icentia11k-continuous-ecg/1.0/) | Open PhysioNet project page. Explicit licence line not retrieved here. [\[59\]](https://physionet.org/content/icentia11k-continuous-ecg/1.0/) | **Very high** for scale and pretraining, especially after a smaller pilot dataset. |
| **PTB-XL** | Record-level diagnosis or weakly supervised event experiments | PhysioNet states PTB-XL contains about **21.8k** clinical **12-lead**, **10-second** ECGs with **71** diagnostic/form/rhythm statements and recommended splits. [\[60\]](https://physionet.org/content/ptb-xl/) | Open PhysioNet project page. Explicit licence line not retrieved here. [\[60\]](https://physionet.org/content/ptb-xl/) | **Medium**. Excellent ECG dataset, but weak fit to DANCE’s event-localisation design unless reformulated. |
| **MIMIC-IV-ECG** | Large-scale auxiliary training or transfer learning | PhysioNet states these are **12-lead**, **10-second** diagnostic ECGs sampled at **500 Hz**. [\[61\]](https://physionet.org/content/mimic-iv-ecg/1.0/) | **Credentialed** PhysioNet access rather than simple open download. [\[61\]](https://physionet.org/content/mimic-iv-ecg/1.0/) | **Medium**. Valuable scale, but poor fit for native event-span supervision and heavier governance friction. |

### EEG-to-ECG replacement table

| DANCE EEG regime | Representative EEG dataset in paper | ECG replacement that preserves the *kind* of event problem | Best candidate |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Many short labelled events per window | Broderick, SparrKULee, Pinet. [\[62\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | Wave delineation or beat events | **LUDB** first, then **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia** or **Icentia11k** |
| Moderate-duration sparse physiological intervals | BNCI2014, BNCI2015. [\[14\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/tangermann2012.yaml) | Sustained rhythm segments rather than motor states | **CPSC2021** |
| Long clinical episodes | TUSZ, TUAR. [\[41\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1) | AF/AFL/VT episodes or long noise episodes | **CPSC2021** and **Icentia11k** |
| Whole-window label tasks | BI2014 P300 variants if reframed coarsely. [\[63\]](https://moabb.neurotechx.com/docs/generated/moabb.datasets.BI2014b.html) | Diagnostic labels over 10-second ECGs | **PTB-XL** or **MIMIC-IV-ECG**, but this is the least DANCE-native use |

If you want a ruthless recommendation rather than a polite list, here it is. **Do not start with PTB-XL** if your real goal is to test whether DANCE’s event-set detector transfers to ECG. Start with **LUDB** if you want exact temporal localisation, or **CPSC2021** if you want clinically meaningful episode spans, then scale to **Icentia11k** once the plumbing works.

## Practical issues, risks, and a migration checklist

Dataset size will dominate your strategy. **LUDB** is tiny and ideal for debugging. **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia** is still small enough for rapid iteration. **CPSC2021** is large enough to test episode detection seriously without becoming a data-engineering monster. **Icentia11k** is the opposite: outstanding for scale, but large enough that the main bottleneck becomes storage, streaming, sharding, window generation, and experiment management rather than the DANCE model itself. **PTB-XL** and **MIMIC-IV-ECG** are large and useful, but because they are mostly fixed 10-second records without native fine event spans, they are better as auxiliary or transfer datasets than as the first direct replacement benchmark. [\[64\]](https://physionet.org/content/ludb/)

Class imbalance is a bigger problem in ECG than the public DANCE release assumes. The EEG code uses plain cross-entropy and a seizure-weighted sampler only for TUSZ. ECG beat and rhythm corpora often have extreme rare-event distributions, so you should expect to add weighted sampling, class weighting, or focal losses early. Do not wait for the validation curve to tell you what you already know. [\[65\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/blob/main/dance/configs/datasets/shah2018.yaml)

Governance and licensing are not side issues. The dance code itself is MIT-licensed, but the paper’s datasets mix open repository datasets, competition data, and registration-controlled TUH corpora. On the ECG side, **MIMIC-IV-ECG** is credentialed, while many PhysioNet datasets are openly accessible. Before you commit to a benchmark, verify that the dataset licence and access mode fit your intended distribution model, especially if you plan to release preprocessed derivatives, trained weights, or a Docker image with cached metadata. [\[66\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

Compute should be analysed honestly. The paper reports average end-to-end runtime and latency across datasets, with typical training on a single **V100** and the seizure task on **8 GPUs**. ECG with fewer channels will often reduce model-side compute relative to 32- or 64-channel EEG, especially if you disable the ChannelMerger. But if you target very dense beat annotations or extremely long ambulatory recordings, the *data pipeline* and *query/event post-processing* become the new bottlenecks. In other words, ECG may be cheaper per sample and more expensive per project. [\[67\]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.10688v1)

The highest-performance risk is not “ECG is different from EEG” in some vague sense. It is that DANCE’s current localisation machinery is **interval-centric**. If you feed it point annotations without redesigning the metric or interval representation, you will get misleadingly bad results and then blame the architecture for a target-definition problem you created yourself. The second major risk is the fixed **256-latent** bottleneck, which can under-resolve short ECG morphology when windows are too long. The third is cross-dataset heterogeneity in leads, filters, devices, and annotation conventions. All three are fixable, but only if you treat them as first-order design constraints. [\[68\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance/raw/refs/heads/main/dance/losses.py)

A practical migration checklist is below.

* Define exactly one first ECG task: **wave delineation**, **beat events**, or **rhythm episodes**.

* Add or wrap an ECG-native study/extractor layer. The retrieved neuralset docs expose EegExtractor, EmgExtractor, MegExtractor, and others, but **no EcgExtractor**. [\[69\]](https://facebookresearch.github.io/neuroai/neuralset/)

* Keep the public DANCE batch contract unchanged initially, even if that means putting ECG under the "eeg" key.

* Disable ChannelMerger for the first baseline.

* Set window length from the **shortest event duration** using the paper’s token-budget logic.

* Convert point annotations to spans or replace IoU-based localisation.

* Replace EEG metrics with ECG-appropriate tolerance and timing metrics.

* Add explicit scipy to dependencies.

* Debug on **LUDB** or **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia** before touching **Icentia11k**.

* Only after you have a stable baseline should you rename symbols, redesign lead geometry, or attempt multi-dataset ECG pretraining.

## Open questions and limitations

Some paper datasets are only partially reconstructable from the retrieved public sources. In particular, I could not fully verify the official source details, licensing, and technical metadata for **BI2013**, **BI2014a**, **BNCI2015**, and **Pinet** from the public sources I accessed here, and the public repo itself only ships runnable configs for **three** examples even though the paper benchmarks **ten** datasets. That is a reproducibility gap worth acknowledging explicitly. [\[70\]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/dance)

For several ECG candidates, I could confirm the core project pages and the main technical characteristics, but I did **not** retrieve authoritative licence text lines for every open PhysioNet dataset in this pass. If you are planning public redistribution, packaging derived labels, or releasing model checkpoints trained on a particular corpus, verify the exact current licence terms on the relevant project page before you ship anything. [\[64\]](https://physionet.org/content/ludb/)

The biggest unresolved engineering question is the neuralset integration layer. The retrieved documentation clearly shows several neuro extractors including EegExtractor, but not an ECG-native one, so the first concrete coding task is likely not inside DANCE’s model at all. It is either building an ECG Study plus extractor in the neuralset ecosystem or bypassing that layer and feeding the standalone Dance module directly from a custom PyTorch dataloader. [\[71\]](https://facebookresearch.github.io/neuroai/neuralset/)

---
